unit.name: Masked Autoencoders as scalable reconstruction pretraining
unit.description: Explains MAE as an asymmetric masked-reconstruction framework built on ViT-style patch encoders, and highlights why it became a dominant pretraining approach.
unit.summary: MAE is introduced as a modern reconstruction-based method that masks a very large portion of image patches and trains a model to reconstruct the full image from the visible subset [ts=2781s] [ts=2825s]. The lecturer explains that images are split into patches like a ViT, around 75% of patches are masked, and only the visible 25% are fed to a large transformer encoder, making the pretext task challenging and allowing many distinct masked views per image during training [ts=2898s] [ts=2960s]. Missing patches are represented with a shared learned mask token before a separate decoder reconstructs the image, so the architecture is explicitly asymmetric and the decoder is used only for reconstruction during pretraining [ts=3009s] [ts=3050s]. Training uses pixel-space mean squared error computed only on masked patches, and the learned encoder can be evaluated via linear probing or full fine-tuning; the lecture also notes that ablations found random masking and roughly 75% masking to work especially well, with MAE outperforming strong contrastive baselines such as DINO and MoCo v3 in the presented comparisons [ts=3062s] [ts=3235s].

key_points.text: MAE masks a large fraction of image patches and reconstructs them from the visible remainder.
The encoder only processes visible patches, typically about 25% of the image after 75% masking.
Masked patches are represented with a shared learned mask token before decoding, yielding an asymmetric encoder-decoder design.
MAE training uses pixel-space mean squared error computed only on masked patches.
Linear probing measures representation quality, while full fine-tuning better reflects the model's downstream potential.
Ablations in the discussion highlight 75% masking and random masking as strong design choices.

kp_catalog.name: Masked Autoencoders (MAE)
kp_catalog.description: A scalable masked-reconstruction approach using a large encoder over visible patches and a lightweight decoder over visible plus mask tokens.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because masked Autoencoders (MAE) synthesizes a scalable masked-reconstruction approach using a large encoder over visible patches and a lightweight decoder over visible plus mask tokens into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Downstream transfer and extrinsic evaluation
kp_catalog.description: Self-supervised methods should be judged mainly by how useful their learned representations are for downstream tasks, often via linear evaluation or fine-tuning. A pre-trained encoder can be reused for downstream tasks by adding lightweight supervised heads such as linear classifiers or decoders.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because downstream transfer and extrinsic evaluation clarifies self-supervised methods should be judged mainly by how useful their learned representations are for downstream tasks, often via linear evaluation or fine-tuning, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Self-supervised representation learning
kp_catalog.description: Momentum-based contrastive methods such as MoCo, DINO, and DINOv2 learn transferable visual features from unlabeled images and scale through queues, EMA encoders, and larger datasets. The encoder is first trained on a pretext task with targets generated from unlabeled data, then transferred to a labeled downstream task.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because self-supervised representation learning clarifies momentum-based contrastive methods such as MoCo, DINO, and DINOv2 learn transferable visual features from unlabeled images and scale through queues, EMA encoders, and larger datasets, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical, high, and medium.
